137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:

207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54

59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an

80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.

144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,

165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}

167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.

177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine

187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses

196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.

255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection}
260	
261	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
262	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
263	  % includes composition)
264	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
265	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
266	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
267	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
268	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
269	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
270	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
271	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
272	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
273	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
274	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
275	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
276	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
277	  canonical projections, and write
278	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
279	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
280	  pullback maps
281	  \[
282	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
283	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
284	  \]
285	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
286	  \[
287	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
288	  \]
289	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
290	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
294	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
295	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
296	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
297	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
298	\begin{proof}
299	  
300	  
301	\leanok
302	  The square
303	  \[
304	    \begin{array}{ccc}
305	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
306	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
307	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
308	    \end{array}
309	  \]
310	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
311	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
312	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
313	  there is a canonical isomorphism
314	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
315	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
316	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
317	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
318	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
319	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
320	  set-valued maps stated above.
321	\end{proof}

323	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
324	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
325	
326	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
327	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
328	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.

330	\begin{theorem}\leanok
331	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
332	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
334	  
335	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
336	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
337	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
338	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
339	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
340	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
341	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
342	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
343	  % topologies  by
344	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
345	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
346	  %  \end{dfn}"
347	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
348	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
349	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
350	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
351	  \[
352	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
353	  \]
354	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
355	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
356	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
357	  a subgroup). Define
358	  \[
359	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
360	  \]
361	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
362	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
364	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
365	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
366	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
367	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
368	\end{theorem}

380	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
381	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.

383	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
384	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
385	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The

388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	\leanok
392	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
393	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
394	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
395	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
396	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
397	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
398	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
399	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
400	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
401	\end{proof}

406	\begin{theorem}\leanok
407	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
408	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
409	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
410	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
411	    lem:pullback_compose, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}

413	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
414	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
415	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
416	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
417	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
418	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
419	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
420	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
421	  %  [\dots]
422	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
423	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
424	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
425	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
426	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
427	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
428	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
429	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
430	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
431	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
432	  \[
433	    \begin{array}{ccc}
434	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
435	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
436	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
437	    \end{array}
438	  \]
439	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
440	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
441	  unique set map
442	  \[
443	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
444	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
445	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
446	  \]
447	  through the quotients of
448	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
449	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
450	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
451	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
452	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
453	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
454	\end{theorem}
455	
456	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
457	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
458	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
459	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
460	\begin{proof}
461	  
462	  
463	\leanok
464	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
465	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
466	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
467	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
468	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
469	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
470	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
471	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
472	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
473	  universal property of the quotient set
474	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
475	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
476	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
477	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
478	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
479	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
480	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
481	\end{proof}
